definition: The act of taking up and holding onto experience, views, habits, or identity.
notes: OSF editorial authority now treats Buddhadasa Bhikkhu as primary for this term, with Dhammarato supporting the practical language of taking up trouble in real time. Clinging remains the default, but the term is now explicitly governed by the OSF reading of appropriation into I and mine rather than by a generic emotional attachment model.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and dependent-origination contexts

context_rules.notes: Acceptable when the motion of taking hold is central.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose tied to the fire metaphor
context_rules.notes: Use selectively because it is not the default doctrinal gloss.

context_rules.context: When the passage emphasizes the transition from craving into the sustaining of becoming
context_rules.notes: Keep the dependent-origination force of taking-up and holding-to explicit.
Notes may explicitly stress appropriation into I and mine.

context_rules.context: In OSF practical dependent-arising explanation of appropriation into I and mine
context_rules.notes: Primary-source-backed default within the OSF line.

context_rules.context: In prose emphasizing active taking-up that fuels becoming
context_rules.notes: Acceptable alternate when the motion of appropriation matters.
